content.claim: Empirical research using administrative data found attending a high-poverty school in Grade 8 is negatively associated with later reading scores and secondary attainment, with no significant association for math; sociological fieldwork identifies self-exclusion among disadvantaged families as driven by parental decision-making during preparation, not the entrance exam alone.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Legal and governance lens
limitations: The causal mechanism splits between formal selection rules and informal parental behavior, limiting what legal reform alone can address
Reading-score association does not extend to all measured outcomes (math shows no effect)
Self-exclusion mechanism suggests even removal of the exam would not eliminate stratification if quality differences and family distrust persist
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
